title: Free Speech and Civil Liberties
text:
Free Speech and Civil Liberties

At a Glance

The United States has a strictly limited federal government, with a robust Bill of Rights, yet our freedom of speech and civil liberties remain in question as government seeks new authority over citizens. The federal government today limits our political speech, subjects us to more wiretapping than ever before, criminalizes more activities every year, regulates and sometimes interferes with the content of broadcasting, and assumes unprecedented police and prosecutorial powers. The best way to protect civil liberties is to rein in the scope and power of the federal government and begin to return the federal government to its constitutional limits.

Join the Conversation

#Cato1A

FISA Section 702 Lapse Assured—What Now?

By Patrick G. Eddington

The real question is whether the House and Senate leadership will finally tell Trump that embracing the Fourth Amendment’s warrant requirement is the only way to end this saga.

Transparency, fairness, clarity, and evidence-driven enforcement should be core components of AI governance.

Events

Free Expression Under Fire from the FCC

Featuring Christoper J. Lewis, Joe Kane, David Inserra, & Jennifer Huddleston

The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) has long exercised significant authority over broadcast media beyond what is typically tolerated under the First Amendment.

Commentary

Taking the Pulse: Are Western Democracies Failing Free Speech?

By David Inserra

Democracies need stronger protections for speech—online and offline—if their citizens are to thrive.

blog

The Government Can’t Mandate Ideological Views in Professional Education

By Thomas A. Berry and Alexander Xenos

Doctors, lawyers, and other professionals must take continuing professional education classes to maintain their licenses. But the government can’t force the teachers of those classes to include ideological speech with which they disagree.
